title: Sheaf-Theoretic Preparation Contextuality
authors: Tom Williams, Mina Doosti, Farid Shahandeh
abstract: We introduce a preparation-dual notion of contextuality, formulated as an obstruction to stochastic extension. In parallel with the sheaf-theoretic formulation of measurement contextuality, preparation contextuality arises when locally specified preparation statistics cannot be extended to a single global response matrix compatible with all source contexts. Whereas measurement contextuality concerns the incompatibility of restriction maps (marginalisation), the preparation setting requires stochastic extension of partial conditioning data, which is inherently non-unique. We identify minimal structural and preparation compatibility conditions on admissible extension matrices and show that they enforce a rigid product form. This leads to a notion of preparation contextuality in which the absence of any admissible global response representation witnesses contextuality, while preparation compatibility identifies the cases in which this obstruction is nontrivial. The framework is formulated explicitly in matrix form and illustrated by a quantum-mechanical example exhibiting preparation contextuality.
summary: This paper introduces a new sheaf-theoretic formulation for preparation contextuality, treating it as a failure of stochastic extension rather than marginalization. It provides a rigorous mathematical framework to identify when local preparation statistics cannot be consistently unified into a single global model. The authors demonstrate the relevance of this theory by proving that the PBR scenario is preparation-contextual.
main_problem: The paper aims to develop a sheaf-theoretic framework for 'preparation contextuality,' a concept dual to measurement contextuality, by defining it as an obstruction to the stochastic extension of local preparation statistics.
main_result: The authors establish that preparation contextuality arises when local preparation data cannot be extended to a global response matrix, and they prove that a specific quantum-mechanical PBR-type scenario exhibits this contextuality.
method: The study uses a linear-algebraic and sheaf-theoretic approach, employing the Kleisli category of the distribution monad and imposing structural constraints like input independence and compositionality on extension matrices.
model_or_system: The framework is applied to a preparation-dual analogue of the Bell scenario, specifically using a PBR-type model involving two laboratories (Alice and Bob) with multiple preparation procedures and a four-outcome POVM.
key_observables: Local and global preparation statistics, empirical models (probability distributions), and the existence of a global response matrix.
important_parameters: The number of sources, preparation instances, and measurement outcomes; the input-independence and compositionality of extension matrices.
main_assumptions: The framework assumes finite sets of measurements and preparations, and requires that extension matrices satisfy input independence and compositionality to ensure coherent refinement.
figures_summary: The notes mention a probability table illustrating an empirical model for the Abramsky-Brandenburger-Bell model and a large matrix representing the empirical model for the PBR scenario.
paper_structure: The paper reviews measurement-based sheaf theory, introduces the preparation-dual framework, establishes mathematical constraints on extension matrices, and concludes with a formal proof of contextuality using a PBR-type example.
why_interesting: It provides a new mathematical foundation for understanding the structural foundations of quantum mechanics, specifically regarding how local preparation procedures relate to global quantum states, which is fundamental to quantum information theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
